summary:
The Battle of Leuctra (371 BC) was a decisive engagement in which the Theban army under the general Epaminondas defeated the supposedly invincible Spartan hoplite army, shattering Sparta's military prestige and ending the [[event:1023|Spartan Hegemony]] over Greece. The battle demonstrated that tactical innovation could overcome the psychological dominance that Sparta had exercised over Greek warfare for generations.

Epaminondas devised a revolutionary oblique order, concentrating his best troops on the left wing in a mass fifty ranks deep rather than the conventional eight — the Sacred Band of Thebes, the elite unit of 300 soldiers linked by sworn brotherhood, led the assault. Against Spartan doctrine, which placed its best troops on the right, Thebes attacked with overwhelming force on the left, striking the Spartan king Cleombrotus's position directly and crushing it before the rest of the battle developed. The Spartan right and center, outmaneuvered and leaderless, fled or were destroyed.

Approximately 400 Spartiates — a catastrophic proportion of Sparta's full citizen body, which had been declining for decades — were killed at Leuctra. The psychological impact was enormous: Greek cities that had submitted to Spartan demands realized the Spartans could be beaten. Thebes followed up with invasions of the Peloponnese that freed the helots of Messenia from Spartan bondage, permanently crippling the economic basis of Spartan military power. The [[event:1021|Theban Hegemony]] that followed lasted only a decade before Greece collapsed into fragmentation, but Leuctra had irreversibly changed the Greek balance of power.
